creator: Sarah Perez
title: (No AI) PayPal says it’s ‘becoming a technology company again.’ That means AI.
dc:creator: Sarah Perez
content: PayPal is pitching an AI-led turnaround, tying automation and restructuring to $1.5B in savings as it cuts jobs and works to modernize its tech stack.
contentSnippet: PayPal is pitching an AI-led turnaround, tying automation and restructuring to $1.5B in savings as it cuts jobs and works to modernize its tech stack.

title: (No AI) OpenAI claims ChatGPT’s new default model hallucinates way less
author: Jay Peters
content:
![](https://platform.theverge.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2026/02/STK155_OPEN_AI_4_CVirginia_A.png?quality=90&strip=all&crop=0,0,100,100)

OpenAI's newest default model for ChatGPT might not make stuff up as much. Hallucinations have [been an ongoing problem](https://www.theverge.com/2024/5/15/24154808/ai-chatgpt-google-gemini-microsoft-copilot-hallucination-wrong) for AI models, but [OpenAI says](https://openai.com/index/gpt-5-5-instant/) its new GPT-5.5 Instant model has "significant improvements in factuality across the board."

The company claims that, based on "internal evaluations," GPT-5.5 Instant produced "52.5% fewer hallucinated claims" than its Instant model for GPT-5.3 "on high-stakes prompts covering areas like medicine, law, and finance." GPT-5.5 Instant also "reduced inaccurate claims by 37.3% on especially challenging conversations users had flagged for factual errors."

OpenAI also claims that GPT-5.5 Instant is …

[Read the full story at The Verge.](https://www.theverge.com/ai-artificial-intelligence/924225/openai-chatgpt-default-model-gpt-5-5-instant)
contentSnippet:
OpenAI's newest default model for ChatGPT might not make stuff up as much. Hallucinations have been an ongoing problem for AI models, but OpenAI says its new GPT-5.5 Instant model has "significant improvements in factuality across the board."
The company claims that, based on "internal evaluations," GPT-5.5 Instant produced "52.5% fewer hallucinated claims" than its Instant model for GPT-5.3 "on high-stakes prompts covering areas like medicine, law, and finance." GPT-5.5 Instant also "reduced inaccurate claims by 37.3% on especially challenging conversations users had flagged for factual errors."
OpenAI also claims that GPT-5.5 Instant is  …
Read the full story at The Verge.
summary: OpenAI's newest default model for ChatGPT might not make stuff up as much. Hallucinations have been an ongoing problem for AI models, but OpenAI says its new GPT-5.5 Instant model has "significant improvements in factuality across the board." The company claims that, based on "internal evaluations," GPT-5.5 Instant produced "52.5% fewer hallucinated claims" than its […]

title: (No AI) Meta sued by major book publishers over copyright infringement
author: Emma Roth
content:
![Vector illustration of the Meta logo.](https://platform.theverge.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/chorus/uploads/chorus_asset/file/25546027/STK043_META_CVIRGINIA_D.jpg?quality=90&strip=all&crop=0,0,100,100)

Meta is facing a class action lawsuit filed by five major book publishers and one author over claims the company "engaged in one of the most massive infringements of copyrighted materials in history" when training its Llama AI models, as [reported earlier by _The New York Times_](https://www.nytimes.com/2026/05/05/books/publishers-turow-meta-zuckerberg-lawsuit-copyright.html?partner=slack&smid=sl-share). In [their suit](https://www.documentcloud.org/documents/28094627-publishers-v-meta/), Macmillan, McGraw-Hill, Elsevier, Hachette, Cengage, and author Scott Turow allege that Meta "repeatedly copied" their books and journal articles without permission.

The lawsuit accuses Meta of knowingly ripping copyrighted work from "notorious pirate sites," such as LibGen, Anna's Archive, Sci-Hub, Sci-Mag, and others, and then feeding that material in …

[Read the full story at The Verge.](https://www.theverge.com/tech/924230/meta-publishers-lawsuit-ai-copyright)

Meta is facing a class action lawsuit filed by five major book publishers and one author over claims the company "engaged in one of the most massive infringements of copyrighted materials in history" when training its Llama AI models, as reported earlier by The New York Times. In their suit, Macmillan, McGraw-Hill, Elsevier, Hachette, Cengage, and author Scott Turow allege that Meta "repeatedly copied" their books and journal articles without permission.
The lawsuit accuses Meta of knowingly ripping copyrighted work from "notorious pirate sites," such as LibGen, Anna's Archive, Sci-Hub, Sci-Mag, and others, and then feeding that material in …
Read the full story at The Verge.

In their suit, Macmillan, McGraw-Hill, Elsevier, Hachette, Cengage, […]

creator: Todd Feathers
title: (No AI) He Couldn’t Land a Job Interview. Was AI to Blame?
dc:creator: Todd Feathers
content: Armed with some Python and a white-hot sense of injustice, one medical student spent six months trying to figure out whether an algorithm trashed his job application.
contentSnippet: Armed with some Python and a white-hot sense of injustice, one medical student spent six months trying to figure out whether an algorithm trashed his job application.
